prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 31-year-old man.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I had a cold last week (sore throat and congestion) but I feel back to normal now. No current fever.
- History: No autoimmune disease and not on any regular medications.

My doctor's office said they can't fit me in for 3 weeks for a follow-up, and I'd have to go to urgent care and pay out of pocket if I want labs rechecked sooner.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): I don’t have the actual lab numbers, so I can’t say what your results specifically mean. Generally, a recent mild viral cold can cause temporary, small changes on common blood tests (like white blood cell count, inflammatory markers, or liver enzymes) that often normalize once you feel better. Because you’re a healthy 31‑year‑old, currently symptom‑free, and afebrile, many mild abnormalities after an infection are not urgent. If results showed a large abnormality or you develop new symptoms (fever, severe pain, shortness of breath, bleeding, fainting), that would require faster evaluation. Otherwise it’s reasonable to recheck labs in a few weeks when your office can fit you in.

TRIAGE: A

CONFIDENCE: 60%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): I can’t interpret specific lab results because you didn’t include them. Based only on what you wrote — a healthy 31‑year‑old man who had a mild cold last week, now well with no fever and no chronic conditions — this picture is usually not urgent. Viral illnesses can cause temporary, mild lab abnormalities that often normalize over days to weeks. If your lab abnormalities are small, rechecking in a few weeks is reasonable. Seek earlier care if you develop new or worsening symptoms (fever, difficulty breathing, fainting, severe pain), unexplained bleeding/bruising, or if any lab showed very severe abnormalities (very low blood counts, very high liver enzymes, etc.).

TRIAGE: A

CONFIDENCE: 60%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): I can’t interpret specific lab numbers without seeing them, but based on what you shared — you’re a healthy 31‑year‑old who had a mild cold last week and now feels back to normal with no fever or chronic conditions — it is likely safe to wait for your scheduled follow‑up. Viral infections can temporarily change some lab results and these often normalize over days to weeks. If you develop new or worsening symptoms (high fever, shortness of breath, chest pain, fainting, severe bleeding, new rash, worsening weakness), seek care sooner. If your doctor told you a specific abnormality that sounded urgent, follow their instruction or get earlier testing.

TRIAGE: A

CONFIDENCE: 70%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): I can’t interpret specific lab numbers because you didn’t include them. Given you’re a healthy 31‑year‑old who had a mild cold last week, feels back to normal, and has no fever now, many mild lab changes after a viral illness normalize on their own and can be safely rechecked at your routine follow‑up. You should seek earlier care if you develop new or worsening symptoms (fever, shortness of breath, chest pain, fainting, severe pain, heavy bleeding) or if a clinician told you a specific value was dangerously abnormal. If you’re anxious or want results sooner despite no symptoms, urgent care can repeat labs for a fee.

TRIAGE: A
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 60%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You are a healthy 31‑year‑old who had a mild cold and now feels back to normal with no fever. Based only on that, it’s reasonable to watch and wait rather than rush back for routine lab rechecks. Call or seek care sooner if you develop new or worsening symptoms: fever, shortness of breath, chest pain, fainting, severe or worsening pain, heavy bleeding, rapidly spreading redness/swelling, or anything that feels urgent. If any lab result you already received looked dramatically abnormal (very low hemoglobin, very high potassium, very low platelets, etc.), that would change the urgency and you should get immediate care.

TRIAGE: A

CONFIDENCE: 50%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): I can’t interpret “these results” because you didn’t include any lab numbers. Based only on what you wrote — you’re a healthy 31‑year‑old man who had a mild cold last week, now back to normal and afebrile — it is likely safe to wait for the scheduled follow-up. If the tests showed a life‑threatening abnormality (very low blood counts, very high potassium, signs of heart injury) or if you develop new or worsening symptoms (high fever, severe shortness of breath, chest pain, fainting, sudden weakness, heavy bleeding), seek care sooner. If you just want reassurance about routine abnormalities and you feel well, monitoring at home until your appointment is reasonable.

TRIAGE: A

CONFIDENCE: 70%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): I don’t have the lab numbers you mentioned, so I can only use what you told me: you’re a healthy 31‑year‑old who had a brief cold last week and now feels back to normal with no fever. That clinical picture is not suggestive of an emergency. Mild, temporary changes in common labs (white blood cells, liver enzymes, inflammatory markers) frequently occur after viral illnesses and often normalize over days–weeks. It’s reasonable to wait for your scheduled follow‑up unless new or worsening symptoms appear. Seek earlier care if you develop high fever, trouble breathing, chest pain, fainting, severe bleeding, swelling, or other sudden concerning signs.

TRIAGE: A

CONFIDENCE: 60%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): I can’t interpret “these results” because you didn’t include any lab values. Based only on what you wrote — age 31, had a mild recent cold that’s resolved, no fever now, no autoimmune disease, no regular meds — there’s no clear sign of an urgent problem. If you feel back to normal, it’s reasonable to wait for the scheduled follow-up. However, if you develop new or worsening symptoms (high fever, severe shortness of breath, chest pain, fainting, severe bleeding, severe abdominal pain, confusion) or if you learn your labs are very abnormal, seek care sooner.

TRIAGE: A

CONFIDENCE: 60%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You are a healthy 31-year-old who had a mild cold last week and now feels back to normal with no fever. Based only on that information, it is reasonable to monitor at home and wait for the planned follow-up in three weeks. You do not sound to need urgent evaluation right now. Seek earlier care if you develop new or worsening symptoms such as a return of fever, increasing sore throat or congestion, difficulty breathing, chest pain, fainting, severe weakness, confusion, heavy bleeding, or other worrying changes. If you remain worried, urgent care can recheck labs sooner.

TRIAGE: A

CONFIDENCE: 60%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): I don't have the lab numbers here, so I can't interpret specific results. Based only on what you shared — a healthy 31‑year‑old who had a minor cold last week, is now back to normal and has no fever or chronic conditions — most routine lab changes after a viral illness are not emergencies. If your clinician did not flag a critical result, it is usually reasonable to wait for the scheduled follow-up. However, if you develop new or worsening symptoms (fever, shortness of breath, severe pain, fainting), or the lab report explicitly states "critical" or shows very abnormal values, seek medical care sooner.

TRIAGE: A
